did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on DenseNet architecture to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset into seven categories (MNV, MLN, BKT, DFM, BCC, ACTK, VSL). The study achieved an accuracy of 90.34% using data augmentation and the Adam optimizer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on DenseNet architecture using the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions. It reports specific results including accuracy (90.34%), validation accuracy (90.87%), and loss metrics, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: A Suitable Approach for Classifying Skin Disease Using Deep Convolutional Neural Network
brief_summary: This study developed a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on DenseNet architecture to classify pigmented skin lesions into seven categories using the HAM10000 dataset. To address class imbalance, the researchers applied data augmentation techniques, generating additional training samples for each original image. The model was trained and evaluated using the Adam optimizer, with images resized to 100x100 resolution. The proposed system achieved an overall testing accuracy of 90.34% and a validation accuracy of 90.87%. The results demonstrate that the CNN model can effectively distinguish between melanoma and other benign and malignant skin conditions. The authors conclude that this approach holds potential for assisting medical professionals in early skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Seven categories of skin lesions (MNV, MLN, BKT, DFM, BCC, ACTK, VSL)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a CNN based on DenseNet architecture to classify pigmented skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset into seven discrete diagnostic categories. The model predicts class labels, achieving an overall testing accuracy of 90.34% and validation accuracy of 90.87%.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine 10000)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, made accessible to the public through the ISIC repository. The dataset contains 10015 images with metadata about patient diagnoses, anatomical locations, ages, and sexes. It is characterized as an imbalanced dataset.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model. After data augmentation (generating 5 additional images for each original image), the training dataset size became 21252 images.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training phase (validation accuracy reported as 90.87%).
use.testing: Used for testing the model. After data augmentation, the testing dataset size became 5313 images. The model achieved an overall testing accuracy of 90.34%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive performance metrics such as accuracy (90.34%) and loss (0.26) without statistical inference.

HAM10000.metric: Testing Accuracy, Validation Accuracy, Testing Loss, Validation Loss, Per-class Testing Accuracy
HAM10000.notes: The model was trained for 50 epochs using the Adam optimizer with a batch size of 128 and learning rate of 0.001. Data augmentation was applied, resulting in 21,252 training images and 5,313 testing images. The validation accuracy fluctuated between epochs 10-20 and 30-40 but steadily rose after epoch 30.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on DenseNet architecture concepts

training_details.data_augmentation: Yes (5 additional images generated for each original image)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to balance the imbalanced dataset (generating 5 additional images for each original image)
Manual alteration for visual contrast and color reproduction (performed on the dataset prior to use)
Cropping and concentrating near the lesion (performed on the dataset prior to use)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset. Five additional images were generated for each original image. This resulted in the training dataset size increasing to 21,252 images and the testing dataset size to 5,313 images.

conclusions: The proposed CNN model based on DenseNet architecture, trained on the HAM10000 dataset with data augmentation, achieved a testing accuracy of 90.34% and a validation accuracy of 90.87% in classifying skin lesions into seven categories.

The model demonstrated the capability to effectively distinguish between melanoma and other benign and malignant skin conditions, with specific per-class testing accuracies ranging from 83% (Melanocytic nevi) to 97% (Vascular lesions).
Data augmentation was identified as a necessary technique to address the class imbalance inherent in the HAM10000 dataset, which otherwise would cause issues in training the CNN model.
The study concludes that the developed system holds potential as a tool to assist medical professionals in early skin cancer diagnosis and classification.

The authors acknowledge that future work should involve refining hyperparameters (such as the number and types of layers) and investigating different pre-trained CNN models to further enhance the design and implementation of the system.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to be imbalanced (e.g., 6705 MNV vs. 115 DFM images); the authors address this via data augmentation (generating 5 additional images per original image) rather than advanced sampling techniques, which may introduce synthetic bias.

The model was trained on images resized to 100x100 resolution, which is significantly lower than the original 600x450 resolution of the HAM10000 dataset, potentially losing fine-grained diagnostic features critical for dermatological classification.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) or confusion matrices, only overall testing accuracy (90.34%) and per-class accuracy, making it difficult to assess performance on minority classes like Vascular lesions (142 images) and Dermatofibroma (115 images).
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the reported accuracy, preventing assessment of the robustness of the 90.34% result.

The architecture is described as 'CNN based on DenseNet' but the layer details (e.g., 11x11 kernel, specific filter counts) do not strictly follow standard DenseNet blocks, raising questions about the fidelity of the architectural description and reproducibility.

The study is a conference paper presented at WCONF 2023, which typically has less rigorous peer review than journals, and the authors acknowledge the need for future work to refine hyperparameters and investigate pre-trained models, suggesting the current implementation may be preliminary.
